title: Dubu Jorim Spicy Braised Tofu

seo.title: Dubu Jorim Recipe: Spicy Korean Braised Tofu (Vegetarian)
seo.metaDescription: Learn to make authentic Dubu Jorim, spicy Korean braised tofu. This easy vegetarian recipe is packed with flavor and perfect for a healthy meal. Get the full recipe!
seo.h1: Authentic Dubu Jorim: Spicy Korean Braised Tofu Recipe (Vegetarian)
h2Headings: Pro Tips for Perfect Braised Tofu
seo.imageAlt: A vibrant plate of Dubu Jorim, spicy Korean braised tofu, garnished with green onions and sesame seeds.
seo.imageCaption: Enjoy a taste of Korea with this delicious and easy Dubu Jorim recipe – perfectly spicy braised tofu!

description: Dubu Jorim is a beloved Korean vegetarian dish featuring pan-fried tofu simmered in a savory, spicy, and slightly sweet sauce. This comforting meal is incredibly flavorful, making it a perfect healthy option for any day of the week. Enjoy this traditional braised tofu with a bowl of warm rice.

ingredients.notes: pressed and sliced into 1/2-inch thick rectangles
sliced, white and green parts separated

instructions.title: Prepare and Pan-Fry Tofu
instructions.text: Press the tofu block for at least 20-30 minutes to remove excess water. Slice the pressed tofu into 1/2-inch thick rectangles. Heat 2 tablespoons of cooking oil in a non-stick pan over medium-high heat. Pan-fry the tofu slices until golden brown and slightly crispy on both sides, about 3-4 minutes per side. Set aside on a plate lined with paper towels to drain excess oil.

instructions.title: Prepare the Braising Sauce
instructions.text: In a small bowl, combine the soy sauce, gochugaru, gochujang, minced garlic, minced ginger (if using), sesame oil, and sugar or rice syrup. Mix well until all ingredients are thoroughly combined and the sugar has dissolved. Set the sauce aside.

instructions.title: Sauté Aromatics
instructions.text: In the same pan (or a clean pan if preferred), add a tiny bit more oil if needed. Add the sliced yellow onion and the white parts of the green onions. Sauté over medium heat until softened and fragrant, about 3-4 minutes.

instructions.title: Arrange Tofu and Add Sauce
instructions.text: Arrange the pan-fried tofu slices in a single layer over the sautéed onions in the pan. Pour the prepared braising sauce evenly over the tofu.

instructions.title: Braise the Tofu
instructions.text: Pour 1 cup of water or vegetable broth into the pan. Bring the liquid to a gentle boil, then reduce the heat to medium-low. Cover the pan and let it simmer for 15-20 minutes, or until the sauce has thickened and the tofu has absorbed the flavors. Occasionally, gently spoon the sauce over the tofu to ensure even coating.

instructions.title: Finish and Serve
instructions.text: Once the sauce has thickened to your desired consistency, remove the lid. Garnish with the green parts of the sliced green onions and toasted sesame seeds. Serve hot with a bowl of freshly cooked rice.

tips: For perfectly firm tofu, press it under a heavy object for at least 30 minutes to remove excess water, which helps it absorb flavor better and prevents crumbling.
Adjust the amount of gochugaru and gochujang to control the spiciness of your Dubu Jorim. For a milder version, reduce the chili flakes and paste.
Dubu Jorim tastes even better the next day as the flavors deepen. Store leftovers in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 3-4 days.
article:
**Dubu Jorim: A Spicy, Savory, and Satisfying Korean Vegetarian Delight**

In the vibrant world of Korean cuisine, where bold flavors and comforting textures reign supreme, vegetarian options sometimes get overlooked amidst the sizzling bulgogi and hearty stews. However, a true gem for plant-based eaters, and indeed for anyone seeking a deeply flavorful and satisfying dish, is Dubu Jorim. This spicy braised tofu dish, simple yet profound in its taste, offers a delightful journey into the heart of Korean home cooking. It’s a testament to how humble ingredients can be transformed into something extraordinary with the right touch of seasoning and technique. If you're looking to expand your vegetarian repertoire or simply crave a comforting, spicy kick, Dubu Jorim is an absolute must-try.

## What is Dubu Jorim?

Dubu Jorim (두부조림) literally translates to "braised tofu." It’s a classic Korean side dish (banchan) or even a main course, featuring pan-fried tofu simmered in a rich, savory, and spicy sauce. The beauty of Dubu Jorim lies in its simplicity and the incredible transformation of plain tofu into tender, flavor-infused bites. Typically, firm or extra-firm tofu is used, which holds its shape well during the braising process. The sauce is a harmonious blend of Korean staples: gochugaru (Korean chili flakes) for heat, soy sauce for umami, garlic, green onions, and sometimes a touch of sugar or rice syrup for balance. The result is a dish that is wonderfully spicy, deeply savory, slightly sweet, and incredibly comforting, perfect for soaking up with a bowl of steamed rice. It’s a staple in many Korean households, cherished for its affordability, nutritional value, and incredible taste.

## Ingredients for Dubu Jorim

Creating authentic Dubu Jorim relies on a few key ingredients, most of which are readily available in Asian markets or even well-stocked grocery stores.

**For the Tofu:**
*   **Firm or Extra-Firm Tofu (1 block, 14-16 oz):** Essential for maintaining shape and absorbing flavor. Avoid silken or soft tofu, as it will fall apart.
*   **Cooking Oil (2-3 tbsp):** For pan-frying the tofu. Neutral oils like canola or vegetable oil work best.

**For the Braising Sauce (Jorimjang):**
*   **Soy Sauce (4-5 tbsp):** The primary savory base. Use a good quality Korean or Japanese soy sauce.
*   **Gochugaru (Korean Chili Flakes) (1.5-2 tbsp):** Provides the signature heat and vibrant red color. Adjust to your spice preference.
*   **Garlic (3-4 cloves, minced):** Adds pungent depth.
*   **Green Onions (2-3 stalks, chopped):** Both for the sauce and as a garnish.
*   **Sesame Oil (1 tbsp):** For nutty aroma and flavor.
*   **Sugar or Rice Syrup (1-2 tsp):** Balances the spice and salt, adding a subtle sweetness.
*   **Water or Vegetable Broth (1/2 cup):** To create the braising liquid.
*   **Optional: Sesame Seeds (1 tsp):** For garnish and added texture.

## How to Make Dubu Jorim (Step-by-Step)

Making Dubu Jorim is a straightforward process that yields incredibly rewarding results. Follow these steps for a perfect batch:

1.  **Prepare the Tofu:**
    *   Drain the tofu block thoroughly. Place it between several layers of paper towels or a clean kitchen towel.
    *   Place a heavy object (like a cutting board with a can on top) on the tofu for at least 20-30 minutes to press out excess water. This is crucial for firming the tofu and allowing it to absorb flavors better.
    *   Once pressed, slice the tofu into 1/2-inch thick rectangles or squares.

2.  **Pan-Fry the Tofu:**
    *   Heat cooking oil in a non-stick skillet over medium-high heat.
    *   Carefully place the tofu slices in a single layer, ensuring not to overcrowd the pan.
    *   Pan-fry for 3-5 minutes per side, until golden brown and slightly crispy. This step adds texture and prevents the tofu from becoming mushy during braising.
    *   Remove the fried tofu from the pan and set aside on a plate lined with paper towels to drain any excess oil.

3.  **Prepare the Braising Sauce:**
    *   In a small bowl, whisk together the soy sauce, gochugaru, minced garlic, half of the chopped green onions, sesame oil, sugar/rice syrup, and water/vegetable broth. Mix well until all ingredients are combined.

4.  **Braise the Tofu:**
    *   In the same skillet (you can wipe it clean if there's too much residual oil, or leave a little for flavor), arrange the pan-fried tofu slices in a single layer.
    *   Pour the prepared braising sauce evenly over the tofu.
    *   Bring the sauce to a gentle simmer over medium heat.
    *   Once simmering, reduce the heat to low, cover the pan, and let it braise for 10-15 minutes. This allows the tofu to fully absorb the delicious flavors of the sauce.
    *   Periodically, you can gently spoon the sauce over the tofu to ensure even coating.

5.  **Finish and Serve:**
    *   Uncover the pan. If the sauce is too thin, you can increase the heat slightly and simmer for a few more minutes, stirring gently, until it thickens to your desired consistency.
    *   Garnish with the remaining fresh chopped green onions and a sprinkle of sesame seeds, if using.
    *   Serve hot with steamed rice.

## Pro Tips for Perfect Braised Tofu

Achieving perfectly flavored and textured Dubu Jorim is easy with a few expert tips:

*   **Pressing is Paramount:** Do not skip pressing the tofu. This step expels excess water, which not only prevents splattering during frying but also creates a firmer texture and allows the tofu to act like a sponge, soaking up all the delicious braising sauce.
*   **Don't Rush the Fry:** Pan-frying the tofu until it's golden brown creates a protective crust that helps it hold its shape during braising and adds a lovely depth of flavor. A slightly crispy exterior contrasts beautifully with the soft, sauce-soaked interior.
*   **Taste and Adjust:** The braising sauce is the heart of Dubu Jorim. Always taste it before pouring it over the tofu. Adjust the spice level (more or less gochugaru), sweetness (sugar/syrup), and saltiness (soy sauce) to your preference.
*   **Even Braising:** Ensure the tofu is arranged in a single layer in the pan so every piece gets ample exposure to the sauce. Gently spooning the sauce over the tofu during simmering also helps.
*   **Don't Overcook:** While tofu is forgiving, braising for too long can sometimes make it overly soft. 10-15 minutes is usually sufficient for the flavors to meld beautifully.

## Serving Suggestions & Pairings

Dubu Jorim is incredibly versatile. It’s most commonly served as a banchan (side dish) alongside a bowl of hot steamed white or multi-grain rice and other Korean side dishes like kimchi, seasoned spinach (sigeumchi namul), or bean sprout salad (kongnamul muchim).

However, its robust flavor and satisfying texture also make it an excellent main course, especially for vegetarian meals. Here are some pairing ideas:

*   **With Rice:** The ultimate classic pairing. The rice acts as a perfect canvas for the spicy, savory sauce.
*   **As a Protein Topping:** Add it to a simple vegetable bibimbap bowl for an extra layer of flavor and protein.
*   **Alongside Soups:** Pair it with a mild soup like a clear vegetable broth or a simple doenjang jjigae (Korean soybean paste stew) to balance the spice.
*   **With Noodles:** While less traditional, chopped Dubu Jorim can be a fantastic addition to a bowl of Korean cold noodles (bibim guksu) or even ramen.

For drinks, water is always a good choice to cut through the spice, but a light lager or even a glass of chilled makgeolli (Korean rice wine) can complement the flavors beautifully.

## Variations and Substitutions

One of the joys of home cooking is the ability to customize. Dubu Jorim is no exception:

*   **Spice Level:** Easily adjust the gochugaru to your liking. For a milder version, use less gochugaru or substitute some with sweet paprika. For extra heat, add a pinch of gochujang (Korean chili paste) to the sauce.
*   **Add Vegetables:** Enhance the dish by braising thinly sliced onions, zucchini, mushrooms, or bell peppers along with the tofu. Add them to the pan a few minutes before the tofu to ensure they cook through without becoming overly soft.
*   **Sweeter Sauce:** For a sweeter profile, increase the amount of sugar or rice syrup in the sauce. A drizzle of honey can also work.
*   **Umami Boost:** A tiny splash of mushroom soy sauce or a pinch of dashi powder (for a non-vegetarian option, or use kombu dashi for vegetarian) can deepen the umami.
*   **Gluten-Free:** Ensure you use tamari or a gluten-free soy sauce.

Dubu Jorim is more than just a dish; it's a celebration of simple ingredients transformed by bold flavors. Whether you're a seasoned chef or a home cook looking for a new and exciting vegetarian recipe, this spicy braised tofu is sure to become a cherished addition to your culinary repertoire. Its comforting warmth and robust taste make it perfect for any meal, proving that vegetarian Korean cuisine is anything but bland.
